notes: Official Monarch product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
” using just your pay stub and 401(k) site, even though Monarch can’t see those exact fields.

I’ll keep it in 3 parts:

What numbers to grab
How to run them through a few rules of thumb
How to read what it all means
1) Numbers to find

Grab a recent pay stub and log in to your 401(k) or HR/benefits site.

From your pay stub

Look for:

Gross pay per period

Labels like: “Gross Pay,” “Regular Earnings,” “Total Earnings.”
Note your pay frequency: weekly, biweekly (every 2 weeks), semimonthly (twice a month), or monthly.

401(k) employee contribution per period

Usually under “Deductions” or “Pre-tax deductions.”
Labels: “401(k),” “401k Pre-tax,” “Roth 401(k),” “Retirement.”
Write down:
The dollar amount this period
The percentage of pay (if shown), like “6%”

Year-to-date (YTD) 401(k) contributions

Same line as above but in a YTD column.
If you use both, add traditional + Roth 401(k) YTD.

Employer match (if shown)

Might show as “Employer 401(k),” “Company match,” or “ER 401(k)” in a YTD column.
If it’s not on the stub, you’ll pull it from the 401(k) portal or benefits booklet.

Your gross annual salary

Might be printed on the stub or in your HR portal.
If not, estimate:
Weekly pay × 52
Biweekly pay × 26
Semimonthly pay × 24
Monthly pay × 12
From your 401(k) website / HR portal

Log in and find:

Current contribution rate

Usually on the home page or under “Contributions” or “Change contributions.”
Note:
% going to traditional 401(k)
% going to Roth 401(k) (if any)

Employer match formula

Often in “Plan Highlights,” “Summary Plan Description,” or “Employer contributions.”
Common formats:
“100% match on the first 3% of pay, then 50% on the next 2%”
“50% match up to 6% of pay”
Write the formula down.

Total retirement balances

401(k) balance (and any old 401(k)s, IRAs, etc., if you can grab them).
A rough total is fine.

Your date of birth / age

From memory or HR profile; you’ll use this for benchmarks and catch-up rules.
2) Plug into simple rules of thumb
A. % of income you’re saving now

Annual employee 401(k) contributions

If you know your contribution %:
Annual 401(k) = Gross annual salary × contribution %
Or use YTD:
If the year is mostly complete, YTD is a good approximation.

Savings rate from salary

Savings rate = (Annual 401(k) contributions ÷ Gross annual salary) × 100
Many people aim for 10–15% of gross income going to retirement (including 401(k), IRAs, etc.). Higher can help if you started later.

Include employer match (optional but helpful)

Total retirement savings rate = (Your contributions + Employer contributions) ÷ Gross salary
B. Are you getting the full employer match?

Use the match formula you wrote down:

Example: “50% match up to 6% of pay”
To get the full match, many people contribute at least 6%.
Compare:
Your current contribution % vs. the % needed to get the full match.

If your % is below the match threshold, a common goal is to increase to at least that level over time.

C. Are you under the IRS limits?

For 2026:

Employee 401(k) limit under age 50: $24,500
Age 50–59 and 64+: extra $8,000 catch-up (total $32,500)
Age 60–63: extra $11,250 catch-up (total $35,750)

Compare your annual 401(k) contributions to the limit for your age.

3) Age-based “am I on track?” check

Once you have your total retirement balances (all 401(k)s, IRAs, etc.) and your current gross annual salary, you can use these common benchmarks:

Age | Typical retirement savings benchmark
---|---
30 | ~1x your annual salary
35 | ~2x
40 | ~3x
45 | ~4x
50 | ~6x
55 | ~7x
60 | ~8x
67 | ~10x

Example:

Age 40, salary $80,000 → benchmark ≈ $240,000 in retirement accounts.
If you’re below the multiple, many people consider increasing contributions toward or above the 10–15% range if possible.
If you’re above, you may already be ahead of typical guidelines.
How to read everything together

Big picture, you’re “doing enough” if:

You’re at least getting the full employer match.
Your total retirement savings rate (including match) is near or above 10–15% of gross income.
Your current total retirement balance is roughly in line with the age-based multiple for your age.

If you want, you can paste the numbers you find (salary, contribution %, YTD contributions, match formula, age, and total retirement balance), and Monarch can walk through the math with you step by step.
